channel.title: Home on The Chopped Computer Scientist
channel.description: Recent content in Home on The Chopped Computer Scientist

item.title: PAMO 2026 P6 thought Process
item.description:
Introduction.

This blog presents my thought process while solving Pan-African Math Olympiad (PAMO P6), at home ofc without contest pressure. The blog assumes you have tried the problem and read the solution or you are currently trying the problem and looking for hints on how to complete it. You can find the problem statement and my solution here : Problem Statement

Guessing the answer.

My first guess was $M$ = 2 for all n, due to the obvious parity invariance. Like most of my quick guesses it was way off. That said if looking at small cases it’s very easy to guess the answer.

item.title: The Permutation Grid
item.description:
Weekly puzzles

Hurray! this is the first in a series of math puzzles that I will writing about in the course of the next few months before I go to college. Hopefully, the procrastination monster doesn’t get me.

Puzzle Statement

Consider a $2025 \times 2025$ grid with the rows numbered $1$ to $2025$ from top to bottom and columns numbered 1 to 2025 from left to right. Each cell of the grid is assigned a number between 1 and 2025 such that the following two conditions hold :
